content: I was writting abot this project for an university project. Stepping into modules, I found that write and read calls need a "borrowed" reference to the device, from the foreignOwnable trait. Why is it needed for a device declared by a module to be stored in C but accessed in Rust? Can't it be stored in Rust? Does it need to be stored in C for the rest of the kernel to be consistent with itself? (Having everything stored in C)
Or maybe it's because there is functionality needed to be performed on the device from C and from Rust?
Usually, when ForeignOwnable is used, it's because C side expects to have a pointer which "own"s something, and we want to track (and generify) such usages for object owned by Rust side.

Then it's used on a Rust variable when there's some functionality needed to be performed on it in C, no? And the pointer in C needs to "own" the memory to be consistent with rust rules? When the C functions are performed, the memory is stored in Rust or in a C object?
